channel.title: Principles of Program Analysis — Audio Notes
channel.description: Private audio notes generated from selected chapters.
channel.itunes:author: Niv Geron
channel.itunes:summary: Private audio notes generated from selected chapters.

image.title: Principles of Program Analysis — Audio Notes

item.title: Four Roads to the Same Truth: An Introduction to Program Analysis
item.description:
In this opening episode, we explore what program analysis really is and why it matters: the art of predicting, at compile time, what a program will do when it runs. Using a single small factorial program as our guide, we walk through the four great approaches—Data Flow Analysis, Constraint Based Analysis, Abstract Interpretation, and Type and Effect Systems—and discover that beneath their different vocabularies, they're often computing the same thing in surprisingly similar ways.

Chapters

  • 00:00 — Opening: why analyze programs before running them
  • 06:04 — Data Flow Analysis
  • 08:09 — Constraint Based Analysis
  • 10:53 — Abstract Interpretation
  • 14:04 — Type and Effect Systems
  • 18:55 — What comes next
item.content:encoded:
In this opening episode, we explore what program analysis really is and why it matters: the art of predicting, at compile time, what a program will do when it runs. Using a single small factorial program as our guide, we walk through the four great approaches—Data Flow Analysis, Constraint Based Analysis, Abstract Interpretation, and Type and Effect Systems—and discover that beneath their different vocabularies, they're often computing the same thing in surprisingly similar ways.

Chapters

  • 00:00 — Opening: why analyze programs before running them
  • 06:04 — Data Flow Analysis
  • 08:09 — Constraint Based Analysis
  • 10:53 — Abstract Interpretation
  • 14:04 — Type and Effect Systems
  • 18:55 — What comes next
item.itunes:summary:
In this opening episode, we explore what program analysis really is and why it matters: the art of predicting, at compile time, what a program will do when it runs. Using a single small factorial program as our guide, we walk through the four great approaches—Data Flow Analysis, Constraint Based Analysis, Abstract Interpretation, and Type and Effect Systems—and discover that beneath their different vocabularies, they're often computing the same thing in surprisingly similar ways.
Chapters: 00:00 — Opening: why analyze programs before running them 06:04 — Data Flow Analysis 08:09 — Constraint Based Analysis 10:53 — Abstract Interpretation 14:04 — Type and Effect Systems 18:55 — What comes next

item.title: Following the Data: How Programs Reason About Themselves
item.description:
In this episode we explore Data Flow Analysis, the classic technique compilers use to understand what a program is doing without actually running it. We'll walk through the four foundational analyses—Available Expressions, Reaching Definitions, Very Busy Expressions, and Live Variables—see how they all fit into a single unifying idea called a Monotone Framework, and learn how a simple worklist algorithm computes the answers. Along the way we'll untangle the vocabulary that trips people up: forward versus backward, may versus must, and how these ideas scale up to whole-program and pointer analysis.

Chapters

  • 00:00 — Opening: why analyze programs before running them
item.content:encoded:
In this episode we explore Data Flow Analysis, the classic technique compilers use to understand what a program is doing without actually running it. We'll walk through the four foundational analyses—Available Expressions, Reaching Definitions, Very Busy Expressions, and Live Variables—see how they all fit into a single unifying idea called a Monotone Framework, and learn how a simple worklist algorithm computes the answers. Along the way we'll untangle the vocabulary that trips people up: forward versus backward, may versus must, and how these ideas scale up to whole-program and pointer analysis.

Chapters

  • 00:00 — Opening: why analyze programs before running them
item.itunes:summary:
In this episode we explore Data Flow Analysis, the classic technique compilers use to understand what a program is doing without actually running it. We'll walk through the four foundational analyses—Available Expressions, Reaching Definitions, Very Busy Expressions, and Live Variables—see how they all fit into a single unifying idea called a Monotone Framework, and learn how a simple worklist algorithm computes the answers. Along the way we'll untangle the vocabulary that trips people up: forward versus backward, may versus must, and how these ideas scale up to whole-program and pointer analysis.
Chapters: 00:00 — Opening: why analyze programs before running them

item.title: Following the Functions: Constraint-Based Control Flow Analysis
item.description:
In this episode, we tackle one of the trickiest problems in analyzing modern programs: figuring out where control actually goes when functions can be passed around as values. We explore Control Flow Analysis for a small functional language, starting from an abstract "acceptability" specification and working our way down to a constraint-based algorithm you can actually run. Along the way we'll meet abstract caches and environments, understand why correctness relies on coinduction, and see how context sensitivity sharpens the results.

Chapters

  • 00:00 — Opening: why analyze programs before running them
  • 18:27 — What comes next
item.content:encoded:
In this episode, we tackle one of the trickiest problems in analyzing modern programs: figuring out where control actually goes when functions can be passed around as values. We explore Control Flow Analysis for a small functional language, starting from an abstract "acceptability" specification and working our way down to a constraint-based algorithm you can actually run. Along the way we'll meet abstract caches and environments, understand why correctness relies on coinduction, and see how context sensitivity sharpens the results.

Chapters

  • 00:00 — Opening: why analyze programs before running them
  • 18:27 — What comes next
item.itunes:summary:
In this episode, we tackle one of the trickiest problems in analyzing modern programs: figuring out where control actually goes when functions can be passed around as values. We explore Control Flow Analysis for a small functional language, starting from an abstract "acceptability" specification and working our way down to a constraint-based algorithm you can actually run. Along the way we'll meet abstract caches and environments, understand why correctness relies on coinduction, and see how context sensitivity sharpens the results.
Chapters: 00:00 — Opening: why analyze programs before running them 18:27 — What comes next

item.title: Seeing Programs Through a Blurry Lens: An Introduction to Abstract Interpretation
item.description:
In this episode we unpack Chapter 4 of *Principles of Program Analysis* — the theory of Abstract Interpretation. We explore how to approximate what a program computes without running it, why complete lattices and monotone functions are the natural setting for this, how correctness relations tie abstract properties to real values, and how widening and narrowing let us tame infinite computations. We finish with Galois connections: the elegant machinery for systematically designing and combining analyses. Along the way we lean on concrete examples like the interval lattice and the lattice of signs.

Chapters

  • 00:00 — Opening: why analyze programs before running them
item.content:encoded:
In this episode we unpack Chapter 4 of *Principles of Program Analysis* — the theory of Abstract Interpretation. We explore how to approximate what a program computes without running it, why complete lattices and monotone functions are the natural setting for this, how correctness relations tie abstract properties to real values, and how widening and narrowing let us tame infinite computations. We finish with Galois connections: the elegant machinery for systematically designing and combining analyses. Along the way we lean on concrete examples like the interval lattice and the lattice of signs.

Chapters

  • 00:00 — Opening: why analyze programs before running them
item.itunes:summary:
In this episode we unpack Chapter 4 of *Principles of Program Analysis* — the theory of Abstract Interpretation. We explore how to approximate what a program computes without running it, why complete lattices and monotone functions are the natural setting for this, how correctness relations tie abstract properties to real values, and how widening and narrowing let us tame infinite computations. We finish with Galois connections: the elegant machinery for systematically designing and combining analyses. Along the way we lean on concrete examples like the interval lattice and the lattice of signs.
Chapters: 00:00 — Opening: why analyze programs before running them
